facts.fact: Quel'Thalas's ward network is experienced physically: a hum around stones, a bass vibration felt in teeth and mounts, and multiple rings of defended magical boundary.
The chapter distinguishes between a forced breach and a disturbance carried through the system itself; Jaina and Cyndia both describe the latter.
Jaina uses chalk work, frost magic, notes, and focus crystals to read the stones as infrastructure carrying damage rather than as isolated magical objects.
The ward barrier at the border post can be keyed open by removing an anchor stone, dropping the damping effect all at once.
The southern wards were muting the contamination; once outside them, the necromantic field is stronger, broader, and physically painful to read.
By chapter2 the corruption is confirmed in soil, shallow water, blood, and living tissue before clean death, not only in leylines or dead bodies.
By chapter3 Jaina can distinguish between strongest concentration and probable first source, keeping Millhaven's loud granary from being mistaken for final answer.
A stone cellar can remain below the taint even inside a saturated village, proving contamination depth is uneven and can be read spatially.
Jaina can temporarily strip magical contamination noise from damaged ground so physical spoor becomes readable again.
Jaina can lay stabilization wards that slow the plague for hours in living patients, buying time without curing the underlying ingestion route.
By chapter4 the plague exists in concentrated liquid form before it is worked through grain, and Jaina reads the preparation as refined necromantic-alchemical craft.
The same quiet necromantic touch can stop a heart and leave a body looking ordinary unless someone knows exactly where to read for it.
A depot can be scrubbed without overt magical masking; ordinary preservation wards on grain may remain while the real concealment happens through paperwork and timing.
Chapter6 refines the plague model: tainted grain teaches the living body over time, death closes the lock, and the final answering force remains unnamed.
Near Andorhal the corruption stops feeling local and begins pressing across wards as a broad ambient sheet, making proximity itself physically punishing to read.

Chapter7 proves the plague's carrier layer is laid onto grain during packaging inside Andorhal, survives milling and baking, and still depends on the living host body to finish part of the transformation.
Small ward markers on specific building faces can matter tactically, changing which approach is safer for a magical read during an operation.
